exemplar.title: Exploring the Starting Bib Numbers of Female Alpine Skiers in World Cup Races through Statistical Calculations

detailedExemplarData.title: Exploring the Starting Bib Numbers of Female Alpine Skiers in World Cup Races through Statistical Calculations

subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Logical progression through introduction, method, results and conclusion
Clear use of headings and sub-headings to guide the reader
Concise and descriptive title and objective statement
weaknesses: Minor lapses in flow (duplicate âInitial Observationsâ sections)
Absence of tables and inconsistent citation formatting
Occasional typos and overly long sentences
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration demonstrates coherent and sustained logical development, matching the band-3 descriptor. Organization is clear and well structured, though not exceptional, and most content is relevant despite some unnecessary repetition. These qualities justify a score of 3 out of 4.

strengths: Use of key statistical terms (correlation coefficient, regression, mean)
Inclusion of scatter plots and regression line representations
An overall logical chain in explanations (plot â compute r â interpret â model)
weaknesses: Notation is sometimes inaccurate or decorative (misuse of \mathbb symbols)
Figures lack axis labels, units, and clear equation labels
Confusion between r, RÂ², and variable roles
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Mathematical language and notation are used at a basic level with some inaccuracies, and two different representation forms are present but not fully exploited. Logical structure is good but marred by clumsy phrasing. These factors align with band-2 performance, meriting 2 out of 4.

strengths: Genuine personal initiative in choosing a self-experienced sport and real FIS data
Clear personal narrative linking the investigation to the studentâs own skiing history
Evidence of creativity in testing alternative models and proposing extensions
weaknesses: Analysis methods remain standard calculator-based procedures
Approach lacks the highly innovative or exceptional personal flair
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work shows significant evidence of independent thinking and a clear personal approach with good creativity. It does not, however, reach the âoutstandingâ threshold. A band-2 score (2 out of 3) accurately reflects this level of engagement.

strengths: Regular meaningful reflection on correlation strength and prediction accuracy
Thoughtful analysis of why linear modelling succeeded or failed
Clear evaluation of outcomes with suggested improvements
weaknesses: Reflection lacks the deep, probing critique for band-3
Some repetition in observations and unprioritized improvement suggestions
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Reflection is meaningful and analytical but stops short of substantial critical depth. Outcomes are evaluated clearly but not comprehensively. This performance aligns with band-2 descriptors, yielding 2 out of 3.

strengths: Correlation and regression calculations are correctly applied to address the research question
Selection of SL-level mathematics (Pearson r, linear/quadratic regression) is appropriate
Step-by-step computations (mean bib number, predicted vs actual) are clear
weaknesses: Confusion in describing dependent and independent variables
Minor errors in interpretation of RÂ² and limited exploration beyond basic techniques
No higher-level or more comprehensive mathematical methods
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Mathematics used is relevant and appropriate but limited in scope. Understanding is good with minor errors, matching band-3 descriptors, yet the scale and depth do not exceed this. A score of 2 out of 6 (mid-band) balances the strengths and weaknesses within the constraints of the total mark.

strandSelections.reasoning: Explanations generally follow a logical chain: describe plot â compute r â interpret â decide on modelling.  While phrasing is occasionally clumsy, the mathematical argument can be followed without confusion.  This reflects âgood logical structure and clear communicationââband 3.

The student correctly computes r and regression coefficients (via calculator) and interprets them sensibly, but confuses dependent/independent variables and misquotes RÂ² once.  Understanding is good with minor errors, matching band 3 (âgood understanding and mostly correctâ).

The student includes scatter plots, a regression line and a brief numerical summary (means).  No tables of raw data appear, and diagrams are of mediocre quality, but at least two representation types are present, so âsome use of different formsâ (band 2) is justified.

The exploration is laid out in a recognisable introductionâmethodâresultsâconclusion sequence and each section follows logically from the previous one.  Ideas progress coherently from the research question through data collection to analysis and findings; the reader seldom needs to infer missing steps.  Minor lapses (e.g. digressions in the observations lists) detract from flawless flow, but overall logical development is clear and sustained, matching the descriptor for 3 marks rather than the âexcellentâ polish demanded for 4.

The student devises a two-stage investigation, tests alternative models, and proposes extensionsâevidence of creativity and initiative above minimal expectations though not âexceptionalâ.  Hence band 2 is appropriate.

The student analyses why the second-run data fails to support linear modelling and interprets r and RÂ² appropriately, indicating thoughtfulâthough not deeply insightfulâcritical analysis of results and method.  Band 2 aligns with this level.

The conclusion contrasts runs, recognises limitations, and suggests improvements and extensions, demonstrating a clear but not comprehensive evaluation of outcomes.  The work meets band 2 descriptors.

Reflection occurs regularly: commentary on strong vs weak correlations, discussion of prediction accuracy, and acknowledgment of external variables.  Depth is meaningful but not critical or probing enough for band 3.  Thus band 2 (âmeaningful reflectionâ) is selected.

Key statistical terms (correlation coefficient, linear regression, r, RÂ², mean) are named and symbols are shown, but notation is sometimes inaccurate (confusing independent/dependent variables, misuse of \mathbb symbols).  Language is occasionally informal yet still mathematical.  Thus âsome appropriate mathematical language and notationâ (band 2) is most fitting.

The narrative is explicitly personalâlinking to the studentâs own racing experience, explaining why SÃ¶lden is meaningful, and interpreting results in that context.  The approach is clearly personal but not highly innovative; band 2 fits best.

Most of the content relates directly to analysing bib number versus rank, yet there is noticeable repetition (two separate âinitial observationsâ sections) and lengthy narrative anecdotes.  Some mathematical symbolism inserted decoratively (\overline{a}, \mathbb{Z}^{\mathbb{Z}}) is irrelevant.  This fits âmostly relevant with some unnecessary elementsâ, corresponding to band 2.

Correlation, regression, mean and r/RÂ² calculations are directly relevant to the question and are applied correctly.  The mathematics is suitable but limited in scope; therefore ârelevant mathematics used appropriatelyâ (3 marks) is most accurate.

Selecting a personally experienced sport and gathering real FIS data show genuine initiative beyond textbook examples.  The choice of two-run comparison indicates some independent framing.  Nonetheless, analysis remains standard (calculator-generated regressions) and does not display outstanding originality, placing it in âsignificant evidenceâ (band 2).

Headings, sub-headings and figures create a clear skeleton; introduction and conclusion are evident; the paper count is reasonable.  However, tables are absent, some figures are duplicated, and references are embedded inconsistently.  These small organisational weaknesses place the work solidly in the âwell organisedâ band but short of the âexceptionally well organisedâ standard.

Pearson r, linear and quadratic regression are all within the SL syllabus and appropriately selected.  No higher-level techniques are attempted.  Hence the mathematics is âcommensurate with course levelâ (band 3).

annotations.comment: The title is clear and descriptive, effectively framing the investigationâs focus on starting bib numbers and race rankings.
Consider incorporating the exact research question or key variables into the title or subtitle to immediately signal the investigationâs scope.
The personal anecdote about skiing from age 3 shows genuine engagement and connects the topic to the studentâs own experience.
The first sentence is very long and contains multiple clauses; consider breaking it into shorter sentences to improve clarity.
You assume constant race conditions; how might variations in weather or course maintenance affect your results?
The main objective statement is concise and clearly outlines the investigationâs aim.
The research question is stated clearly, but the phrase âtop female skierâ is ambiguous; specify whether this applies to all competitors or a subset.
The description of using official FIS data is appropriate and ensures the reliability of your dataset.
Typographical error: âvearâ should be corrected to âyearâ to maintain professionalism.
Define explicitly which variable is \(x\) and which is \(y\) when introducing correlation and regression to avoid ambiguity.
Figure 1 lacks axis labels and units, which undermines clarity of representation.
Excluding non-finishers is justified and improves the validity of your analysis.
Decorative symbols like \(\overline{a}\) and \(\mathbb{Z}^{\mathbb{Z}}\) do not clarify your observations; remove or replace with precise notation.
Avoid having two separate âInitial Observationsâ sections; consolidate to improve conciseness and flow.
When introducing \(r\), show the formula \(r=\frac{\sum(x-\bar{x})(y-\bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum(x-\bar{x})^2\sum(y-\bar{y})^2}}\) to strengthen mathematical communication.
There is a confusion when describing dependent and independent variables in regression; clarify which is \(x\) and which is \(y\).
Label your regression equation clearly, e.g. â\(y=1.02x+3.22\)â with a description of variables.
The scatterplot in Figure 4 should include axis labels, a title, and a key for the regression line to enhance interpretation.
Comparing predicted versus actual bib differences is insightful and shows good use of the model.
Present the example calculation in a proper equation environment for clarity, e.g. â\(x=5\implies y=1.02(5)+3.22=8.32\)â.
Figure 3 (second run) would benefit from overlaying the fitted regression curve to illustrate model performance.
The observations for the second run repeat themes from Run 1; consider focusing on new patterns or contrasts.
The mean formula is correct but uses \(f(x)\) which is undefined here; replace with \(x_i\) notation: \(\bar{x}=\tfrac1n\sum x_i\).
The step-by-step calculation of the mean bib number (19.4) is clear and well-presented.
Your rationale for not fitting a linear model with \(r=0.673\) is sound but could cite a threshold or significance test for justification.
In the context of correlation you use â\(\pm\)â incorrectly; clarify whether you mean both positive and negative relationships.
Notation confusion: \(r^2\) and \(R^2\) are used interchangeably, and \(\mathbb{R}^2\) appears mistakenly; standardize your notation.
The conclusion effectively summarises findings but would benefit from deeper reflection on practical implications for competitive strategy.
The limitations section demonstrates a thorough critical evaluation of external factors affecting the model.
In limitation 2, you note random bib assignment among top 15; consider quantifying its impact by modelling a random variable.
Your proposed improvements are comprehensive but could be prioritized to guide immediate next steps.
Works Cited vary in format; adopt a consistent citation style (e.g. APA or MLA) and ensure all entries include authors and access dates.
